metadata.summary: Tech and niche audience ads via follower lookalikes, keyword targeting, native-style copy, CPM/CPC budgets, and biweekly creative refresh.

content.title: Twitter/X Ads
content.summary: Twitter/X advertising for tech and niche audiences: choose the right objective (Website Traffic or Conversions for most campaigns), target via follower lookalikes and keyword conversations, write native-looking copy in hook/value/CTA format, set realistic CPM/CPC budgets, and refresh creatives every two weeks. The core rule is: use follower lookalike targeting against specific @accounts rather than broad interest categories — Twitter's interest graph is less precise than Meta's, but follower overlap is a strong intent signal.

why.p: Twitter/X reaches audiences that are actively discussing specific topics in real time, making it valuable for tech products, developer tools, and content promotion — but only when targeting is precise. Broad interest targeting on Twitter produces high CPMs with poor conversion rates; follower lookalikes of relevant accounts (competitors, media, influencers) narrow to users with proven topic affinity. Overly polished creative underperforms because it breaks the native feed experience — conversational, text-heavy ads consistently outperform visual-first formats on this platform.

list.item: Reaching tech-savvy, developer, or founder audiences not efficiently reachable via Meta or LinkedIn
Promoting content (newsletters, reports, tools) to engaged niche communities
Real-time event or launch campaigns tied to trending topics
Building follower base for an organic Twitter/X strategy
Retargeting website visitors via Twitter pixel tailored audiences
E-commerce with broad consumer targeting — Meta's audience graph is far superior for consumer products
B2B with job-title targeting requirements — use LinkedIn Ads for role-based targeting
Video-first awareness campaigns — YouTube/Meta Reels provide better video completion rates and lower CPV
Campaigns requiring detailed conversion optimization — Twitter's algorithm has less conversion data than Meta or Google

content.note: X Ads API access has been gated since 2023; many community SDKs broke. Confirm API tier and price before promising automation. Conversion attribution on X is weaker than Meta/Google — pixel coverage drops when users open links in the in-app browser. Audience sizes are smaller than reported; follower lookalikes of an account with 100K followers may yield a usable pool of only 10-30K active users in a region. Disapproval reasons are vague; appeals are slow and largely manual. Engagement metrics inflate reported engagement rate but rarely correlate with conversions.

section.name: targeting-methods
section.p: Follower lookalike, keyword, conversation, and tailored audience targeting with effective combinations.

rule.statement: Use follower lookalike targeting against 3-5 specific @accounts as the primary targeting method — never rely solely on Twitter's broad interest categories for B2B or tech products.
rule.rationale: Twitter's interest taxonomy is coarser than Meta's; follower overlap against specific accounts (competitor, media, influencer) is a far more precise intent signal for niche tech and professional audiences.

list.item: Interests — predefined category-level targeting (lower precision)
Follower Lookalikes — users similar to followers of specified @accounts (high precision)
Keywords — users who tweeted or engaged with specific words in the past 7 days
Conversation Topics — users engaged in specific trending conversations
Events — audiences around live events and moments
Tailored Audiences — your own data (website pixel, email list, app)
Demographics — age, gender, location
Tech/SaaS: Follower lookalikes (@stripe, @linear, @productboard) + Interests: Startups, Technology + Location: US
Newsletter: Keywords (relevant topic terms) + Follower lookalikes (@media accounts) + Interests: Entrepreneurship
Retargeting: Tailored Audience (website pixel visitors) + Demographics
B2B: Follower lookalikes (@industry leaders) + Keywords (job-related terms)

rule.statement: Use keyword targeting to reach users who recently tweeted or engaged with your topic — combine with follower lookalikes to narrow to the right segment.
rule.rationale: Keyword targeting alone casts too wide a net (anyone who mentioned the word); combining with follower lookalikes ensures the keyword match occurs within an already-relevant audience.

section.name: creative-and-copy
section.p: Ad formats, specs, copy structure, and native vs polished creative rules.

list.item: Image — 1200x675px (16:9) or 1:1; standard promotion
Video — 0:15-2:20 duration, max 512MB; demos and engagement
Carousel — 2-6 images or videos; multiple products or steps
Text only — 280 characters; simple direct messages
App card — image + app install button; mobile apps

rule.statement: Structure all Twitter/X ad copy as: Hook (stop scroll) → Value (what they get) → CTA (what to do) — never lead with brand name or product name.
rule.rationale: Twitter feed scrolls at high speed; the first line determines whether users stop. Brand-first openers are ignored; problem/benefit-first openers interrupt scrolling behavior.

rule.statement: Use conversational, text-heavy creative that looks like an organic tweet — avoid highly designed visuals with logos and taglines in the image.
rule.rationale: Twitter users have strong ad-blindness to polished promotional imagery. Native-looking ads blend into the timeline and receive 2-3x higher engagement rates than design-heavy formats on this platform.

list.item: Good: text-first, problem-aware, conversational tone, minimal branding in image
Bad: brand logo prominent, stock photo hero, marketing headline over image

template.purpose: Three ad copy templates: problem/solution, social proof, value-first

section.name: budget-and-measurement
section.p: Cost benchmarks, bidding strategy selection, and key metric targets for Twitter/X campaigns.

list.item: CPM: $5-15 (lower than Meta/LinkedIn for broad audiences)
Cost per follower: $2-5
Cost per engagement: $0.25-0.50 target

rule.statement: Start with a minimum of $20/day; use $50+/day for meaningful testing data within 7 days.
rule.rationale: Below $20/day, impressions are too sparse to generate statistically valid A/B comparison data between ad variations in a reasonable timeframe.

list.item: Automatic bid — recommended for new campaigns; lets Twitter find efficient delivery
Maximum bid — set per-click ceiling; use when CPCs exceed acceptable threshold
Target bid — optimize toward a specific average cost; use after 2 weeks of baseline data
Engagement rate: 1% average, 3%+ good
CTR: 0.5% average, 1%+ good
Cost per engagement: under $0.50, under $0.25 is excellent
CPC: under $2.00, under $1.00 is excellent

rule.statement: Refresh creative every 2 weeks — Twitter audiences are more homogeneous than Meta's, causing ad fatigue faster due to high impression repetition within small niche audiences.
rule.rationale: Niche follower-lookalike audiences on Twitter can be as small as 50-200K users; at $50/day, frequency reaches 3+ within 10-14 days, after which engagement rates drop sharply.

list.item: Install Twitter pixel on all pages
Configure conversion events (purchase, signup, lead)
Add UTM parameters to all destination URLs
Monitor replies — Twitter ads generate comments that require active moderation

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A marketing agent picks angles and competitor follower lists; a content agent drafts native-style tweets (no "ad voice"); a publishing agent uploads via X Ads API or n8n. Because attribution is weak, the post-launch agent's main job is to compare on-platform CTR/CPC against landing-page conversion logs from the site's own analytics — never trust X-reported conversions alone. Iterate weekly: refresh creative every 14 days, kill anything below 0.5% CTR after $30 spend.

Recommended subagents: faion-ads-agent (runs X Ads API mutations, pulls insights), faion-content-marketer (drafts tweet-native copy; doesn't sound like an ad), faion-improver (weekly diff: on-platform CTR vs site-side conversions vs spend).

Best practices: lead with a tailored audience (email list) for retargeting before any prospecting — X's pixel coverage is too thin for cold scaling. Use follower lookalikes of competitors with 50K+ followers for usable pool. Never use boosted-tweet style ad creative — make it look like an organic post. Cap frequency at 2 impressions per user per week; audiences fatigue fast. Always include a Twitter pixel + UTMs and verify with site-side analytics. Test 5+ creatives at launch; expect to kill 4 of them.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Most common mistakes in Twitter/X advertising with explanations.

antipattern.description: Targeting broad interest categories without follower lookalike refinement.
antipattern.reason: Twitter's interest taxonomy is imprecise; broad interests produce high CPMs with low conversion rates because the audience pool includes tangentially interested users.

antipattern.description: Using highly polished, design-heavy creative with prominent brand elements.
antipattern.reason: Polished ads are immediately identified as advertisements and scrolled past; native-looking conversational ads achieve 2-3x higher engagement on Twitter's feed.

antipattern.description: Launching only one ad variation per campaign.
antipattern.reason: No variation means no optimization path; Twitter's algorithm cannot identify the best creative, and a single underperforming ad exhausts budget with no recourse.
Negative or unanswered replies appear under the ad to all subsequent viewers; unmonitored negative sentiment actively damages conversion rates for the entire campaign duration.

antipattern.description: Running the same creative for more than 3 weeks to small niche audiences.
antipattern.reason: Frequency fatigue in small audiences (under 500K) degrades engagement rates and inflates CPCs; creative must be rotated before the audience burns out.

template.purpose: Pre-launch checklist: objective, targeting, creative, budget, tracking
